content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Disclosure: I work at ZoopCoder, an Indian agency that builds e-commerce stores. So I sell the thing this article is about, and there is a section near the end arguing some of you should not buy it.
Every conversation I have with a founder about building an online store starts in the same place: what will it cost to build? It is the wrong first question, and asking it in that order is how people end up with a store that is quietly unprofitable.
The build is a one-time number. In India it is ₹40,000 to ₹90,000 for a properly configured Shopify or WooCommerce store — real design pass, product variants, GST-compliant invoicing, courier integration, a working payment gateway. That is our published price, not a market estimate. Custom-coded stores with checkout logic or an ERP integration run ₹1,50,000 to ₹5,00,000. Below about ₹40,000 you are buying a theme installation, not a store.
Fine. Now here is the number nobody puts next to it.
The checkout can cost more per year than the build did once
Shopify has its own payment processor, Shopify Payments. In countries where it operates, using it means Shopify takes no transaction fee — you pay the card rate and nothing else.
It does not operate in India.
So Indian merchants connect a third-party gateway — Razorpay, PayU, Cashfree. And Shopify charges an additional transaction fee on every order that runs through one: 2% on Basic, 1% on Grow, 0.6% on Advanced. That is on top of whatever the gateway already takes, which for a standard Razorpay account is about 2% on domestic cards.
An Indian store on Shopify Basic can pay roughly 4% on a card order, where a merchant in a Shopify Payments country pays about 2%. Double, structurally, for reasons that have nothing to do with how well the business is run.
Put a revenue number on it. At ₹50,00,000 of annual sales, that extra 2% is ₹1,00,000 a year. The store cost ₹40,000 to ₹90,000 to build, once. The surcharge costs more than that, every year, forever.
That is the whole point. The store is not the cost. The orders are. A build is a number you negotiate once; a percentage is a number that compounds quietly with every success you have. Growth makes it worse, not better — which is precisely backwards from how founders assume costs behave.
(Shopify’s India plan pricing and Razorpay’s rates are third-party figures checked in July 2026. They move. Verify with the vendor before planning around them.)
Where the lines cross, with the arithmetic in public
The usual framing — “Shopify costs money, WooCommerce is free” — is not the comparison. The two platforms bill you in different currencies. Shopify takes a fixed subscription plus a percentage of sales. WooCommerce takes nothing for the software but needs hosting and continuous security maintenance, which is priced in labour.
Shopify’s cost grows with revenue. WooCommerce’s stays roughly flat. So the lines cross, and you can find exactly where:

• ₹5,00,000/yr in sales — Shopify ~₹31,200 (Basic ₹21,200 + 2% fee ₹10,000) vs WooCommerce ~₹42,000 (hosting ₹6,000 + ₹3,000/mo plan). Shopify wins.

• ₹15,00,000/yr — Shopify ~₹51,200 vs WooCommerce ~₹48,000. WooCommerce, just.

• ₹50,00,000/yr — Shopify ~₹1,21,200 vs WooCommerce ~₹1,21,000. A dead heat.

• ₹1,00,00,000/yr — Shopify ~₹1,79,300 (Grow) vs WooCommerce ~₹1,36,000. WooCommerce.
You can derive the crossover yourself rather than trusting my table. Shopify Basic is ₹21,200 a year including GST, plus 2% of sales. A ₹4,000/month WooCommerce arrangement is ₹48,000 flat. Set them equal: 21,200 + 0.02S = 48,000, so 0.02S = 26,800, so S = ₹13,40,000.
Below roughly ₹13.4 lakh a year in sales, Shopify is cheaper. Above it, WooCommerce is — with one condition that matters more than the arithmetic: only if you are actually paying somebody to maintain it. If you genuinely do your own updates and backups, WooCommerce is cheaper everywhere and this comparison collapses. If nobody does them, you do not own a cheaper store. You own a security incident on a timer.
The upgrade that does not pay for itself
When the 2% starts to hurt, the instinct is to upgrade to Grow and halve it to 1%. Check it first.
Grow costs roughly ₹58,000/year more than Basic and saves 1% of sales. It pays for itself only when 1% of sales exceeds ₹58,000 — about ₹58,00,000 in annual sales. Below that, staying on Basic and paying the higher percentage is cheaper. No plan comparison page frames it that way, because framing it that way sells fewer upgrades.
Zero MDR does not mean zero
“UPI has zero MDR” is true, and I have watched it wreck more unit-economics models than any other single sentence in Indian e-commerce.
Zero MDR is a regulatory cap on the merchant discount rate — the interchange the banks would take. That genuinely is nil.
Your payment gateway is a different business. It charges a platform fee for the dashboard, reconciliation, refunds and settlement — commonly around 2%, plus 18% GST on the fee itself.
Zero MDR means the bank takes nothing. It does not mean your gateway takes nothing. If you have a thin-margin catalogue and you modelled UPI orders as free, the model is wrong in the direction that hurts.
And cash on delivery, while we are here: no gateway fee, but a COD handling charge plus full two-way shipping on every refused order. It is the most expensive way to get paid in India and it presents itself as the cheapest.
The costs that are not the platform at all
Platform choice is the argument everyone enjoys having. It is rarely the biggest line item.

• Product photography — ₹150 to ₹800 per product. Avoidable only if you shoot it yourself, and it will look like you shot it yourself.

• Catalogue data entry — descriptions, variants, weights, HSN codes. Several days per few hundred SKUs. Not avoidable; someone does it, either you or someone you pay.

• Paid apps and plugins — ₹1,000 to ₹10,000 a month once you start installing them. Entirely avoidable. Install nothing until a real problem demands it.

• Returns and RTO on COD orders — two-way shipping plus packaging on every refusal. Reducible with partial-prepaid; not avoidable.

• Domain and SSL — ₹800 to ₹1,500 a year, and never pay for a basic SSL certificate. Let’s Encrypt is free and is what most of the internet runs on.
When you should not build a store
We build these for a living, so weigh this accordingly. These are still real, and I would rather say them before an invoice than after one.

• Fewer than about ten SKUs and no repeat customers. A WhatsApp catalogue and a payment link will outperform a store, at roughly zero cost.

• You have not sold the product anywhere yet. Sell thirty units through Instagram DMs first. If you cannot sell thirty by hand, checkout is not your constraint.

• Your margin cannot absorb ~2% plus shipping plus returns. Some catalogues genuinely cannot. Discover that on a spreadsheet, not three months after launch.

• Your volume is already on Amazon or Flipkart and you are happy. An own-brand store is a marketing project, not a technical one. The website is the easy part.

• Nobody will own the catalogue. A store with stale stock and wrong prices is worse than no store, because it disappoints people who were ready to buy.
The short version

• Building a store in India: ₹40,000 to ₹2,50,000 for the overwhelming majority of businesses.

• Running one: ₹31,000 to ₹1,36,000 a year, excluding the gateway percentage you pay either way.

• Shopify Payments does not operate in India, so Indian merchants pay Shopify 2%/1%/0.6% on top of a gateway’s ~2%.

• WooCommerce becomes cheaper above roughly ₹13.4 lakh in annual sales — if someone is being paid to maintain it.

• Basic to Grow does not pay for itself until about ₹58 lakh in annual sales.

• Zero MDR caps the bank’s cut, not your gateway’s.
Ask what an order costs before you ask what the store costs. The first number sets your margin for as long as the business exists. The second one you pay once and forget.
Full breakdown — build price bands by store type, timelines, the four-revenue-level running-cost table and the FAQ: How much does an e-commerce website cost in India? (2026). Our published price list is at zoopcoder.com/pricing.php if you want to check the figures I quoted for our own work.
